title: This Is the Oldest Amber Ever Found
gist: A study published in Science Advances identifies amber that dripped from a tree in China roughly 385 million years ago as the oldest amber ever found, pushing back the known origin of tree resin by about 65 million years.

insights.claim: A study published in Science Advances documents amber that dripped from a tree in China roughly 385 million years ago, the oldest amber ever found and about 65 million years older than the previous record.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Extends the fossil record's amber-based window on prehistoric life by tens of millions of years, opening new preservation-based research targets for that period.
insights.locator: Anyone who's seen Jurassic Park can tell you

insights.claim: Amber can trap and preserve insects, lizards, dinosaur feathers, and pollen, giving paleontologists near-pristine snapshots of prehistoric life.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Establishes why amber finds are scientifically valuable, the benchmark against which future 'oldest amber' discoveries will be judged.
insights.locator: Anyone who's seen Jurassic Park can tell you

insights.claim: Because the fossil amber record only goes back as far as the evolutionary origin of resin itself, dating the oldest known amber effectively dates the origin of resin production in plants.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Ties the amber-based fossil record's outer limit directly to plant evolutionary history, giving researchers a concrete handle for tracking when resin production first evolved.
insights.locator: Of course, these snapshots only go back

insights.claim: Resin is a chemically unique plant substance: when a tree is injured it exudes terpenoid and/or phenolic compounds that form long polymer chains and harden on contact with air.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Documents the biochemical mechanism that has to exist before amber can form, relevant context for evaluating future oldest-amber claims.
insights.quote: it exudes terpenoid and/or phenolic compounds that form long polymer chains, hardening when they contact air
insights.locator: Resin isn't just dried tree sap

insights.claim: Hardened tree resin functions like a biological scab, sealing wounds and protecting the tree from microbes, insects, and further damage.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Frames resin production as an evolved defensive adaptation, the functional reason natural selection favored the trait whose fossilized byproduct (amber) is being dated.
insights.locator: These hard resins act like a sort of scab
